title: Photon bunching: when intensity correlations told us something about light that interference could not
intro: Robert Hanbury Brown was a radio astronomer at Jodrell Bank in the mid-1950s with a strange new instrument: two photomultiplier tubes pointed at the same star, their photocurrents fed into a correlator that multiplied the intensity fluctuations of one signal by the fluctuations of the other. He and his theoretician collaborator Richard Q. Twiss used it in 1956 to measure the angular diameter of Sirius at about 7 milliarcseconds, by reading off the way the cross-correlation between the two photocurrents fell as the telescopes were pushed apart on a circular rail. The optical-physics community was outraged: prominent letters in Nature and Soviet journals insisted that photons could not be 'bunched' in flight and that the experiment had to violate quantum mechanics. The instrument turned out to measure a quantity the Michelson amplitude interferometer cannot read at all, the second-order correlation function g²(τ), and it separated thermal light from a laser even when both had unit first-order coherence, putting Roy Glauber's 1963 quantum theory of optical coherence (later the 2005 Nobel) on a footing the laboratory could test.

sections.heading: 1. Hanbury Brown and Twiss, 1956: measuring a star with intensity, not amplitude

blocks.text: Hanbury Brown had cut his teeth on radio interferometry. By 1949 at Jodrell Bank he had measured the angular size of the supernova remnant Cassiopeia A by intensity correlation between two radio receivers several kilometers apart: each receiver's rectified output was multiplied with the other's, the product low-pass-filtered, and the resulting correlation as a function of receiver separation told him how big the source was on the sky, by the same diffraction logic as a Young double slit but using intensities rather than amplitudes. The technique was forced on him by the practical impossibility of phase-locking two radio receivers a few kilometers apart in the 1940s. The intensity correlator did not care about phase; it only cared about the slow envelope of the noise on each photocurrent.

In 1955 Hanbury Brown and Twiss proposed extending the trick to optical wavelengths. The proposal was to point two large light buckets (each a 6.5-foot ex-army searchlight mirror with a photomultiplier at the focus) at a bright star, run each photocurrent through a high-pass filter to keep only the rapid fluctuations, multiply the two signals together, and integrate. The angular diameter of the star would be read off the way the correlation died as the two mirrors were pushed apart on a circular rail. They built the full instrument at Narrabri in New South Wales over the next decade; the pilot results, taken at Jodrell Bank on Sirius, came in 1956. The angular diameter they reported was 6.9 milliarcseconds, in agreement with the value inferred from Sirius's known distance and assumed surface brightness.

The community of optical physicists pushed back hard. The objection (most loudly raised in correspondence in Nature and in the Soviet journals) was that the intensity correlator implied photons in the two beams 'knew about each other,' which seemed to contradict quantum mechanics: a photon detected at one photomultiplier was annihilated and could not transmit any influence to the photon arriving at the other a meter away. Edward Mills Purcell, then at Harvard, replied in a now-famous Nature letter of 1956 showing that for thermal light (light coming from a vast number of independent atomic emitters with random phases, the kind a hot filament or a star produces) the photon arrivals must, by elementary quantum statistics of indistinguishable bosons, be bunched in time. The bunching is a property of the source field itself, not a post-hoc effect of detection, and the Brown-Twiss correlator was reading off a real quantity that Michelson's amplitude stellar interferometer could not see.

What did the instrument actually read? Let I(t) be the optical intensity (power per unit area) at the detector. A photon-counting detector with quantum efficiency η registers single-photon clicks at an instantaneous mean rate proportional to I(t). Send a beam through a 50/50 beam splitter (an optical element that transmits half the incident intensity and reflects half) onto two detectors A and B, and ask: given a click on A at time t, what is the probability density of a click on B at time t + τ? Up to a constant prefactor that does not depend on τ, this joint rate is proportional to the time-averaged product ⟨I(t) I(t+τ)⟩, where the angle brackets denote a long-time average over the random fluctuations of the source. Normalizing by the product of the mean intensities gives the second-order correlation function:

blocks.caption: g²(τ) is the normalized second-order correlation function of the optical intensity at time delay τ. I(t) is the instantaneous intensity at the detector at time t, in units of power per unit area. ⟨I(t) I(t+τ)⟩ is the long-time average of the product of intensities at two times separated by τ, the autocorrelation of I. ⟨I(t)⟩² is the square of the mean intensity, the denominator that makes g²(τ) dimensionless and independent of overall flux. The angle brackets denote averaging over the random fluctuations of the field. The case τ = 0 measures the intensity correlation at coincident times: how often a high reading at the detector is accompanied by a second high reading at the same instant.

blocks.text: Pull out the τ = 0 case and rewrite it in terms of the variance of I. Use the identity ⟨I²⟩ = ⟨I⟩² + Var(I), divide both sides by ⟨I⟩², and notice that the first piece becomes 1 and the second piece becomes the normalized variance of the intensity:

blocks.caption: g²(0) is the second-order correlation at zero delay, the value of g²(τ) at τ = 0. ⟨I²⟩ is the mean of the squared intensity. ⟨I⟩² is the square of the mean intensity. Var(I) = ⟨I²⟩ − ⟨I⟩² is the variance of the intensity. The relation says g²(0) equals exactly one plus the normalized variance of the intensity, the variance measured in units of the squared mean. A field with no intensity fluctuations at all (Var(I) = 0) has g²(0) = 1. A field whose intensity fluctuates substantially has g²(0) greater than 1, and the excess g²(0) − 1 is exactly the relative variance.

blocks.text: The Hanbury Brown-Twiss correlator forms the product I_A(t) × I_B(t + τ) on the two detectors after the splitter, averages it, and divides by the product of the mean intensities. The number it reports is exactly g²(τ). Three distinct kinds of light (thermal, coherent, and antibunched) give three distinct g²(0) values, which is why the same starlight that Michelson's amplitude interferometer cannot distinguish from a perfectly coherent laser source gets correctly tagged as thermal by the intensity correlator. The next section walks the three cases.

quiz.question: An imaginary laboratory source has a perfectly constant intensity I(t) = I₀ at all times (no fluctuations at all). What does g²(0) equal for this source, and what physical content does that number carry?
options: g²(0) = 0, because there are no fluctuations and therefore no correlations between the two detector clicks at coincident times.

g²(0) = 1, because Var(I) = 0 makes the formula g²(0) = 1 + Var(I)/⟨I⟩² collapse to 1. The number 1 corresponds to clicks arriving independently at a constant rate, the joint coincidence rate being exactly the product of the single-detector rates. A constant-intensity field would be the classical limit of a perfectly coherent source.
g²(0) = 2, because the two detectors see the same source field and their clicks are perfectly correlated.
g²(0) is undefined for a constant field because the autocorrelation of a constant is itself a constant rather than a function of τ.
quiz.explanation: Option 0 mistakes 'no correlation' for 'g² = 0,' but the normalization of g² is by ⟨I⟩², which is finite, so g²(0) for a stable nonzero field is finite and equal to ⟨I²⟩/⟨I⟩² = ⟨I₀²⟩/⟨I₀⟩² = 1. Option 2 confuses 'same source' with 'extra coincidences,' but a source with no fluctuations has no excess coincidences to give, even when both detectors see the same field. Option 3 mistakes the smoothness of the autocorrelation for a divergence; the function is perfectly well defined and equals 1 at every τ. The honest answer is option 1. A constant intensity has zero variance, the formula gives g²(0) = 1 + 0 = 1, and the physical meaning is that coincidences arrive at the rate predicted by the product of the single-detector rates with no excess and no deficit. This is the same g²(0) a laser produces well above threshold, and that match is not a coincidence: an ideal laser is the closest physical realization of a 'constant intensity, fluctuating only with the inevitable photon shot noise' field. The 1 is the baseline against which thermal sources (g²(0) = 2) excess and quantum-emitter sources (g²(0) < 1) deficit are measured.

sections.heading: 3. Three regimes: thermal, coherent, and antibunched

blocks.text: The first regime is thermal light: blackbody radiation, the light from a filament lamp, the light from a star. The intensity comes from a vast number of independent atomic emitters with random phases, so the electric field E(t) at any one point is a sum of many uncorrelated random contributions. By the central limit theorem the real and imaginary parts of E(t) are Gaussian-distributed with zero mean. The intensity I(t) = |E(t)|² is then a sum of two squared independent zero-mean Gaussians, which has an exponential distribution. An exponential distribution has the convenient property Var(I) = ⟨I⟩², so the formula from the previous section evaluates immediately:

blocks.caption: g²_thermal(0) is the second-order correlation at τ = 0 for a thermal (chaotic) light field. Var(I) is the variance of the intensity for such a field. ⟨I⟩² is the square of the mean intensity. The exponential distribution of I has Var(I) = ⟨I⟩² exactly, so the ratio Var(I)/⟨I⟩² equals 1. Adding 1 gives g²(0) = 2. The same result, written more abstractly, is the Siegert relation g²(0) = 1 + |g¹(0)|² = 1 + 1 = 2, valid for any field with Gaussian field statistics. The factor of 2 is the famous photon-bunching excess: a thermal source delivers twice the joint coincidence rate at τ = 0 that an independent-emission model would predict.

blocks.text: The bunching dies away at delays longer than the coherence time τ_c of the source. Inside τ_c the random field amplitude has not yet shuffled to an uncorrelated value, so a high I(t) is statistically accompanied by a high I(t + τ); outside τ_c the two are independent and g²(τ) decays to 1. For a chaotic source with Lorentzian spectral lineshape, g²(τ) = 1 + exp(−2|τ|/τ_c); for a Gaussian lineshape, g²(τ) = 1 + exp(−(πτ/τ_c)²). The bunching peak is sharp and concentrated, which is why Hanbury Brown and Twiss had to push the temporal resolution of their photomultiplier electronics close to the natural coherence time of the spectrally-filtered starlight to see a measurable excess.

The second regime is the laser well above threshold. The field is in what Roy Glauber, in three Physical Review papers in 1963, called a coherent state: an eigenstate of the photon annihilation operator that gives a Poissonian photon-number distribution and behaves, in the classical limit, as a wave of perfectly definite amplitude and phase. The intensity has no excess noise beyond the unavoidable shot noise of photon counting, and the second-order correlation is flat at unity:

blocks.caption: g²_coherent(τ) is the second-order correlation function of a coherent state (laser well above threshold) at delay τ. τ is the delay between the two detector clicks. The value is 1 at every τ, not just at τ = 0. The flat unity says coherent-state photons arrive completely independently of one another, exactly as the events of an ideal Poisson process: the probability of a click in any small interval is unchanged by knowing that an earlier click occurred. There is no bunching peak at zero delay and no dip; the laser is the calibration source against which both thermal excess and antibunched deficit are measured.

Three simulated photon-counting streams: thermal (chaotic), coherent (laser), and antibunched (single quantum emitter). Pick a source with the buttons, then watch the click train scroll across the upper timeline. The lower panel accumulates the empirical g²(τ) histogram: pair coincidences at delay τ, normalized by the product of single-detector rates, so the asymptote at long τ is 1 for all three sources. Thermal grows a bump at τ ≈ 0 climbing toward 2; coherent stays flat at 1; antibunched cuts a hole at τ ≈ 0 toward 0. The coherence time τ_c slider sets the width of the thermal bump and the recovery time of the antibunched dip. The histogram refreshes every few seconds as the simulator runs more pairs.

blocks.text: The third regime is what no classical wave can produce: a field with g²(0) < 1, an antibunched source. The only way to make one is with a single quantum emitter (one isolated atom, one trapped ion, one self-assembled semiconductor quantum dot) that holds at most one excitation at a time. After the emitter releases a photon, it must absorb another pump photon to re-excite before it can radiate again. The dead time τ_d between successive emissions is set by the excited-state lifetime, and the probability of two clicks arriving within τ_d is suppressed far below the random-coincidence rate. The cleanest case, a single two-level atom in steady-state resonance fluorescence, has g²(0) = 0 in the weak-driving limit, recovering to 1 at delays much longer than the excited-state lifetime.

sections.heading: 4. Why first-order coherence cannot tell them apart

blocks.text: Michelson's interferometer reads a different quantity. Send a beam into a Michelson setup, recombine the two daughter waves with a path difference cτ, and the fringe visibility V(τ) at the output is exactly the modulus of the first-order correlation g¹(τ) of the input field. The fringe visibility is the ratio (I_max − I_min)/(I_max + I_min) of the bright-fringe to dark-fringe intensities as a function of the path difference, and the first-order correlation is the normalized autocorrelation of the electric field amplitude itself:

blocks.caption: V(τ) is the fringe visibility at the output of a Michelson interferometer when the two arms are unbalanced by a path difference cτ, where c is the speed of light. g¹(τ) is the normalized first-order correlation function of the input optical field at delay τ. E(t) is the complex electric-field amplitude at the input at time t. E*(t) is its complex conjugate. ⟨E*(t) E(t+τ)⟩ is the average of the product of the field at two times separated by τ. ⟨|E(t)|²⟩ is the mean intensity. The vertical bars on g¹ take its modulus; the visibility is necessarily real and nonnegative. V(0) = 1 for any source, and V(τ) decays on the timescale τ_c of the field's first-order coherence.

blocks.text: Now consider a disturbing fact. A spectrally filtered thermal source can be given an arbitrarily long coherence time τ_c by narrowing its spectral filter (filtered sunlight through a Fabry-Perot etalon, for example, can reach τ_c of nanoseconds). Its g¹(τ) decays with characteristic time τ_c, identical in shape to a laser's. A laser well above threshold, with phase noise from spontaneous emission, also has finite τ_c. There is no fringe-visibility experiment, no matter how long the Michelson arms or how narrow the source spectrum, that distinguishes a spectrally-matched thermal source from a coherent laser source at the same τ_c. The two produce identical fringes.

But the second-order statistics are completely different. Thermal light at τ = 0 has g²(0) = 2 by the Siegert relation; coherent light has g²(0) = 1 by construction. The Hanbury Brown-Twiss correlator can tell them apart in a single measurement, while no Michelson can. The deeper formal statement, from Glauber's 1963 papers, is that g¹ depends only on first-order moments of the field operators (the operator combination â†(t) â(t+τ), normal-ordered) while g² involves second-order moments (â†(t) â†(t+τ) â(t+τ) â(t), again normal-ordered). Different operator combinations probe different aspects of the photon-number distribution at a mode, and knowing g¹ to all orders does not pin down g².

This is the cleanest case in optics of a hidden parameter showing itself in a higher moment. A filtered thermal source and a laser, prepared to have the same spectrum, the same intensity, and therefore the same first-order coherence, are indistinguishable by any classical-amplitude measurement. The g² statistic exposes a fact about the photon-number distribution underlying the field: thermal photons cluster (exponential photon-number distribution at a single mode), coherent photons do not (Poissonian distribution), and the cluster vs. no-cluster distinction is invisible to amplitude interferometry. Hanbury Brown and Twiss had built a probe that opened a new axis in the parameter space of light, and it took Glauber's quantum theory of coherence in 1963 to lay out the axis cleanly.

quiz.question: Two unmarked tabletop sources, A and B, sit on a bench. Both emit at 633 nm with the same intensity. You send each in turn through a Michelson interferometer and measure fringe visibility V(τ) as a function of path delay; both sources give identical Lorentzian-decay visibility curves with the same coherence time τ_c = 1 ns. What single additional measurement would distinguish a filtered thermal source from a helium-neon laser, and what numerical signature would each give?
options: Measure the spectrum on a high-resolution spectrometer; the laser will have a Voigt lineshape and the thermal source will have a Lorentzian, and they can be separated by the wings.

Run the beam from each through a Hanbury Brown-Twiss correlator (50/50 beam splitter, two photon-counting detectors, coincidence counter as a function of delay τ) and compute the second-order correlation g²(τ). The thermal source gives g²(0) = 2 dropping to 1 at delays larger than τ_c = 1 ns, while the laser gives g²(τ) = 1 flat at all τ. The factor-of-2 excess in coincident clicks at τ = 0 is the photon-bunching signature, and it is invisible to any Michelson measurement because both sources have identical first-order coherence g¹(τ).
Measure each source's average intensity stability over an hour; the laser will be more stable.
Try to phase-lock each source to a reference cavity; only the laser will lock, because thermal light has no coherent phase.
quiz.explanation: Option 0 is wrong both about lineshapes (a HeNe laser well above threshold has a Lorentzian linewidth set by spontaneous emission, and a filtered thermal source has whatever shape its filter imposes) and in principle: the question explicitly stipulated that both sources have the same τ_c, which means the same spectral width and the same g¹(τ). A high-resolution spectrum measures g¹ as a Fourier transform, so it returns the same answer for both. Option 2 is a statement about long-time amplitude stability, not about the photon statistics that g² measures; you can have a perfectly intensity-stabilized thermal source (e.g., a feedback-locked filtered lamp) whose g²(0) is still 2. Option 3 confuses the macroscopic phase of a coherent state with the microscopic random phase of a thermal field; a phase-lock measurement is essentially another way to read g¹, and again returns the same answer. The honest answer is option 2. The HBT correlator computes g²(τ) directly: send each source onto a 50/50 splitter, send each output to a photon-counting detector, histogram the pair-coincidence delays τ, and normalize by the product of single-detector rates. The thermal source gives a peak at τ = 0 of height 2 (Var(I) = ⟨I⟩² for chaotic light, so g²(0) = 1 + 1 = 2), falling back to 1 at delays of order τ_c = 1 ns, while the laser gives a perfectly flat g²(τ) = 1. The factor-of-2 excess is the photon-bunching signature, and it is precisely the quantity Michelson cannot read because Michelson measures g¹ and bunching lives in g².

sections.heading: 5. 1977: Kimble, Dagenais, and Mandel cross g²(0) = 1 from below

blocks.text: By the mid-1970s the Hanbury Brown-Twiss correlator had become a routine spectroscopic and atomic-physics tool, and the experimental search for a g²(0) < 1 source (an unambiguous quantum signature in the light field itself, not just in the matter that emitted it) was open. The challenge was practical. Any classical fluctuating field, however exotic, gives g²(0) at least 1: a classical intensity is a nonnegative number and the variance is nonnegative, so by g²(0) = 1 + Var(I)/⟨I⟩² it cannot go below 1. To see g²(0) < 1 you need a source that actually emits one photon at a time with strong dead-time correlations between successive emissions. A single isolated two-level emitter is the textbook case. Single trapped ions had been demonstrated by Hans Dehmelt's group at Washington in 1973, and single-atom traps for neutral atoms were under development by Vladimir Letokhov's group in Moscow, but stable photon-by-photon optical detection of fluorescence from one atom in a beam was still demanding work in 1977.

H. Jeff Kimble, Mario Dagenais, and Leonard Mandel set up the experiment at the University of Rochester. They prepared a low-density atomic beam of sodium, crossed it with a dye laser tuned to the sodium D₂ resonance line at 589 nm (the same yellow line whose doublet Michelson had used a century earlier to calibrate the standard meter), and arranged the photomultipliers and laser beam so that the fluorescence collection volume contained on average much less than one atom at any one instant. Pair-coincidence histograms of the resonance-fluorescence photons were accumulated over multi-hour runs. The data showed a clear dip in g²(τ) centered at τ = 0, with g²(0) ≈ 0.4 (limited by their inability to fully suppress contributions from runs in which two atoms momentarily occupied the volume), rising back to 1 at delays of order the natural sodium-D₂ excited-state lifetime, 16 ns. The 1977 paper in Physical Review Letters reported g²(0) below 1 by 7.5 standard deviations.

The interpretation is decisive in a way the 1956 measurement was not. The Hanbury Brown-Twiss bunching of thermal light at g²(0) = 2 is a quantum-statistical effect in the sense that it requires bosonic indistinguishability for its derivation, but it can also be reproduced by a classical model of light in which the intensity is a fluctuating nonnegative random variable: any such classical model gives g²(0) at least 1 and can hit exactly 2 with the right intensity distribution. No classical model of light gives g²(0) less than 1. The Kimble-Dagenais-Mandel measurement was therefore the first experimental fact about light that could only be explained by treating the electromagnetic field itself as a quantum object, with photon-number observables that admit Wigner functions taking negative values over regions of phase space. After that paper, quantum optics existed as a distinct field with its own quantitative tests, separable from atomic physics and from semiclassical radiation theory, and the practical search for ever cleaner single-photon sources (today: nitrogen-vacancy centers in diamond, quantum dots in microcavities, parametric down-conversion heralded pairs) had its founding measurement to point back to.

finalQuiz.question: A student claims that because the Michelson interferometer can measure the fringe visibility V(τ) of any optical source and recover its first-order coherence g¹(τ), and because g¹(τ) is the Fourier transform of the source's spectrum, the Michelson measurement fully characterizes the photon statistics of the source and there is no further information to extract. Where does this reasoning go wrong, and what does the Hanbury Brown-Twiss measurement of g²(τ) actually add?
options: The student is correct in principle, but the Michelson measurement is in practice limited by photon shot noise and the HBT correlator is just a different way of getting around that noise floor; both measurements ultimately recover the same information about the source.

The student is right that g¹ characterizes the spectrum, but wrong that the spectrum determines the photon statistics. Photon statistics live in g²(τ), and g² is independent of g¹ in the sense that two sources with identical g¹(τ) can have completely different g²(τ): a filtered thermal source and a laser with the same τ_c give identical Michelson fringes but differ by a factor of 2 in g²(0). The HBT correlator measures the variance of the photon-number distribution at the source, exposing whether photons cluster (thermal, g²(0) = 2) or arrive independently (coherent, g²(0) = 1) or actively repel each other (single quantum emitter, g²(0) < 1). The Kimble-Dagenais-Mandel observation of g²(0) ≈ 0.4 at sodium D₂ in 1977 is the cleanest existing demonstration that g² carries information no g¹ can ever supply, because no classical model of light can give g²(0) < 1, while every classical model of light can match any g¹(τ).

The student is wrong because the Michelson measurement requires phase-locking, and only laser sources can be phase-locked; thermal sources cannot be measured with Michelson at all, which is why HBT exists.
The student is wrong because g¹(τ) is in fact determined by g²(τ) (the field is a square root of the intensity), so the Michelson measurement is redundant once you have HBT.
finalQuiz.explanation: Option 0 misses the actual content: the two correlators measure formally different operator moments (g¹ ~ ⟨â†(t)â(t+τ)⟩, g² ~ ⟨â†(t)â†(t+τ)â(t+τ)â(t)⟩), and you cannot recover one from the other in either direction. Option 2 is wrong on a basic fact: Michelson works perfectly well with thermal sources (Michelson himself measured the standard meter in cadmium light), and the fringe visibility of a filtered thermal source is exactly the modulus of its g¹(τ). Option 3 inverts the relation: g¹ and g² are independent quantities, and no functional that takes g² as input gives g¹ as output, or vice versa. The honest answer is option 1. The student's mistake is the implicit assumption that the spectrum (which Michelson recovers via g¹) determines the photon statistics. It does not. The variance of the intensity is a second-moment property of the field, and the simplest demonstration is the filtered-thermal-vs-laser pair: both can be prepared to have the same spectrum, the same τ_c, and the same g¹(τ), and they differ by exactly a factor of 2 in g²(0). The HBT correlator measures g²(τ) directly and adds the photon-number-distribution axis to the description of light: bunched (g²(0) > 1, thermal), independent (g²(0) = 1, coherent), or antibunched (g²(0) < 1, single quantum emitter). The antibunched case is the most consequential, because no classical model of light can produce it. The 1977 Kimble-Dagenais-Mandel observation of g²(0) ≈ 0.4 from a single sodium atom in resonance fluorescence is therefore the cleanest experimental fact about light that requires treating the field itself as a quantum object, and it sits beyond the reach of any conceivable Michelson measurement.
